edge: Deep reinforcement learning works impressively in some environments and fails catastrophically in others.
RL theory should be able to provide an understanding of why deep RL works well in some environments and fails in others, i.e. bounds predictive of practical performance.
We compare standard deep RL algorithms to prior sample complexity bounds by introducing a new dataset, BRIDGE.
BRIDGE consists of 155 deterministic MDPs from common deep RL benchmarks, along with their corresponding tabular representations.
Deterministic environments share many interesting properties of stochastic environments, but are easier to analyze.
When actions with the highest Q-values under the random policy also have the highest Q-values under the optimal policy, deep RL tends to succeed; when they don’t, deep RL tends to fail.
We generalize this property into a new complexity measure of an MDP that we call the effective horizon.
The effective horizon roughly corresponds to how many steps of lookahead search would be needed in that MDP in order to identify the next optimal action.
When leaf nodes are evaluated with random rollouts, the effective horizon can be determined.
BRIDGE is used to show that the effective horizon-based bounds are more reflective of the empirical performance of PPO and DQN than prior sample complexity bounds.
The effective horizon is shown to be more reflective of the empirical performance of PPO.
The effective horizon is shown to be more reflective of the empirical performance of DQN.
It is found that the effective horizon can predict the effects of using reward shaping.
It is found that the effective horizon can predict the effects of using a pre-trained exploration policy.

A new theoretical complexity measure for MDPs, called the effective horizon, is introduced that satisfies certain criteria.
The effective horizon measures approximately how far ahead an algorithm must plan in an environment before evaluating leaf nodes with random.
An algorithm must exhaustively plan in an environment before evaluating leaf nodes with random, as measured by the effective horizon.
To assess previous bounds and arrive at certain properties, a new dataset named BRIDGE of deterministic MDPs from common deep RL benchmarks is created.
The dataset BRIDGE includes deterministic MDPs from common deep RL benchmarks for analysis.
Deterministic MDPs from common deep RL benchmarks are used to compute their tabular representations.
Tabular representations involve computing over 100 million states of deterministic MDPs for exhaustive exploration and storage.
The focus is on deterministic MDPs in BRIDGE to connect theoretical and empirical results at a significant scale.
Focus on deterministic MDPs in BRIDGE aids in their analysis with practical implications.
MDPs described in the context have many interesting properties like reward sparsity and credit assignment challenges, similar to stochastic processes.
MDPs discussed in the context exhibit properties like reward sparsity.
MDPs mentioned in the context are associated with credit assignment challenges.
Many deep RL benchmarks mentioned are nearly deterministic, affecting their relevance to practical RL.
The analysis of deep RL benchmarks is considered highly relevant to practical RL.
The environments in BRIDGE play a crucial role in the analysis of deep RL benchmarks.
BRIDGE represents a set of environments where a surprising property for learning optimally exists.
The identification of a surprising property in many environments in BRIDGE indicates that learning to act optimally can occur by acting randomly.
Actions with the highest Q-values under a uniformly random policy also have the highest Q-values under the optimal policy, highlighting their significance.
Actions that have high Q-values under a random policy are aligned with the optimal policy, indicating a relationship between the two policies.
PPO, a popular deep RL algorithm, efficiently solves environments where the identified property holds.
PPO's success in solving environments is linked to its ability to efficiently use Q-function information.
Environments where the property of learning optimally by acting randomly exists are efficiently solved by the PPO algorithm.
The Q-function associated with the random policy provides valuable information for successful applications in deep RL algorithms.
Modern algorithms appear to simplify to acting greedily on the Q-values of the random policy within environments in BRIDGE.
Acting greedily on the random policy Q-values implies the property that it is optimal to act greedily with respect to the random policy’s Q-function.
Acting greedily with respect to the random policy’s Q-function implies that very simple algorithms designed to estimate the random policy’s Q-function could efficiently find an optimal policy.
The property that it is optimal to act greedily with respect to the random policy’s Q-function has important implications for RL theory and practice.
Practically, it suggests that very simple algorithms designed to estimate the random policy’s Q-function could efficiently find an optimal policy.

We introduce such an algorithm, Greedy Over Random Policy (GORP), which also works in the case where one may need to apply a few steps of value iteration to the random policy’s Q-function before acting greedily.
Empirically, GORP finds an optimal policy in fewer timesteps than DQN (another deep RL algorithm) in more than half the environments in BRIDGE.
GORP is an adaptation of a Monte Carlo planning algorithm to the reinforcement learning setup.

In most BRIDGE environments, effective horizon H is significantly less than horizon T

RL (Reinforcement Learning) and Monte Carlo rollout algorithms are related as both are algorithms used in reinforcement learning. Monte Carlo rollout algorithms are sometimes referred to as 'one-step' or 'multi-step' lookahead, which is a key concept in RL.
One-step lookahead and multi-step lookahead are related terms used in the context of RL algorithms, specifically in the discussion of GORP and Monte Carlo rollout algorithms.

One-step lookahead and value iteration are related as a few steps of value iteration often lead to fast convergence to optimal policies, which is equivalent to a step of Newton's method for finding a fixed-point of the Bellman equation.
Deep RL (Deep Reinforcement Learning) and GORP are related as the success of deep RL is attributed to similar properties found in GORP, which approximates a step of policy iteration in model-free RL.

MDP (Markov Decision Process) refers to a mathematical framework used for modeling decision-making processes in an environment. The state space size is a key parameter in defining the complexity of the MDP.

The effective planning window (EPW) and effective horizon are related concepts in MDPs. The EPW aims to capture the same intuition as the effective horizon, where W = 1 in one case and W = T in another case.
Rewards beyond the planning window are ignored in the analysis based on the EPW. This limitation can affect the performance of RL algorithms.
The effective horizon-based bound is compared between different MDPs (Markov Decision Processes) to show its ability to account for the rewards that are beyond the planning window.

Sample complexity bounds based on the effective horizon can predict the empirical performance of deep RL (Reinforcement Learning) algorithms more accurately when compared to other bounds in the literature.
The BRIDGE dataset is used to run deep RL algorithms on each MDP (Markov Decision Process) to understand the empirical sample complexity and performance.

PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization) and DQN (Deep Q-Network) are two deep RL algorithms used to determine the optimal policy in MDPs. PPO converges in 95 of 155 MDPs, while DQN converges in 117 of 155.
